title: Background
summary: This page discusses the historical development of complex numbers, highlighting Karl Friedrich Gauss's proof of the fundamental theorem of algebra in 1799 and his interpretation of complex numbers as points in a complex plane. It explains how the number system was broadened to include imaginary numbers, with real numbers becoming a special case of complex numbers. An analogy using two neighboring countries is introduced to illustrate the utility of complex numbers, accompanied by Figure B.1.

figures.caption: Two routes between points a and b crossing the border between Country X and Country Y, illustrating a direct route versus a longer alternate route that stays within one country, used to motivate the concept of analytic continuation in the complex plane.
figures.description: The figure shows a rectangular map-like region with no axes, divided by a curved border into shaded Country X and unshaded Country Y. Two labeled points, a and b, lie in Country X; a straight line marked "Direct route" goes from a to b and crosses Country Y, while a dotted curved line marked "Alternate route" stays entirely within Country X. Students should notice the contrast between the shorter path that leaves the country and the longer path that avoids crossing the boundary, illustrating path choice relative to a border.
